title: TOPIK I Authentic Mock Exam 01 — Guided Course

courses.title: Greetings and Everyday Short Answers
objectives: Answer yes/no questions by echoing the verb (이에요?, 있어요?)
Reply to simple wh- and frequency questions
Use the right set phrase for first meetings and farewells

new_grammar.explanation: Polite question asking whether something or someone is N; answered with 네, N이에요 or 아니요, N이/가 아니에요.
Asks whether N exists or whether the listener has N; answer with 있어요 or 없어요.
Marks the place where an action happens; required in answers to 어디에서 questions.

courses.review: You can now field basic yes/no, wh-, and greeting exchanges by matching the verb and the set phrase.

courses.title: Places Around Town
objectives: Identify a place from the verbs and items in a short dialogue
Tell apart a library, hospital, shop, and station
Say what you want to do with -고 싶다

new_grammar.explanation: Expresses the speaker's desire to do something.
Marks the endpoint of movement or a range (to/until).
Adjective modifying a noun: 따뜻하다 → 따뜻한 코트.

courses.review: Let the verb decide the place: 빌리다 means a library, 아프다 means a clinic, and the vehicle word names the station.

courses.title: Talking About a Topic
objectives: Spot the umbrella topic that covers both speakers' key words
Talk about seasons, family, hobbies, birthdays, age, jobs, and weather
Give a reason with -아/어서 and connect nouns with 하고

new_grammar.explanation: Connects a reason to a result: A-아/어서 B = because A, B.
Connects two nouns (and); colloquial equivalent of 와/과.
Means 'when (doing/being)': 시간이 있을 때 = when there is time.

courses.review: Topic questions reward the category both sentences share — find what is said, not just which words you heard.

courses.title: Particles and Word Choice
objectives: Choose the object marker 을/를 for 좋아하다 and the location marker 에 for 있다/없다
Pick the verb that collocates with its object (노래를 부르다)
Connect cause and result with 그래서

new_vocabulary.en: to not exist, to not be (somewhere)

new_grammar.explanation: The liked thing is the object of 좋아하다 and takes 을/를.
Existence/location uses the particle 에.
Marks a point in time after a duration or event.

courses.review: Match the particle to the verb and the verb to its object — those collocations decide the fill-in-the-blank items.

courses.title: Shopping and Weather Situations
objectives: Match a dialogue to the right scene, watching who plays which role
Track each speaker's plan and catch adverbs like 벌써
Make polite requests (-아/어 주세요) and state obligations (-아/어야 되다)

new_grammar.explanation: Requests that someone do an action for the speaker.
Expresses necessity or obligation (have to).
Expresses a future plan or intention.

courses.review: In situation and detail items, pin down who does what — speaker-swaps and flipped adverbs are the usual traps.

courses.title: Reading Notices and Tickets
objectives: Read a class notice, store sign, or movie ticket for time, place, and price
Check each option against the notice when the task asks for what is NOT correct
Use conditions (-(으)면) and (im)possibility (-(으)ㄹ 수 있다/없다)

new_grammar.explanation: Expresses duration: 두 시간 동안 = for two hours.
Condition: if/when A, then B.
new_grammar.example: 만 원 이상 사면 배달해 드립니다.

new_grammar.explanation: Can / cannot do something.

courses.review: Notices hide the answer in exact numbers — verify floors, prices, and a.m./p.m. character by character.

courses.title: Opinions and Main Ideas
objectives: Find the speaker's overall point, separating it from supporting reasons
Keep two disagreeing speakers' views apart
Use concession (-아/어도), comparison (N보다), and nominalization (-는 것)

new_grammar.explanation: Even if/though - concedes a point without changing the conclusion.
Comparison particle: than N.
Nominalizes a verb: V-ing.

courses.review: The main idea is the conclusion, not the supporting reason — in short essays it is often the last sentence.

courses.title: Everyday Narratives
objectives: Follow a short personal story and reorder its sentences by time and logic
Catch the reason someone gives and which person it applies to
Contrast clauses with -지만 and mark beneficial actions with -아/어 주다

new_grammar.explanation: Contrast connector (but/although).
Doing an action for someone's benefit.
Attached to a time noun, means 'every': 아침마다, 주말마다.

courses.review: Order a story by its broadest time word first, and remember 그래서 always needs a cause before it.

courses.title: Longer Reading Passages
objectives: Read a multi-sentence passage and recognize paraphrases of its facts
Predict a blank from discourse markers (그런데, 그래서) and the problem→solution arc
Use decisions (-기로 하다), long negation (-지 않다), and noun-based reasons (N 때문에)

new_grammar.explanation: Expresses a decision made about a future action.
Long-form negation of verbs and adjectives.
Because of N - noun-based reason, common paraphrase of -아/어서.

courses.review: Longer passages test paraphrase recognition and the problem→solution arc — the final blank usually resolves the opening problem.
